reviews.title: smooth flow with good line weight
reviews.text:
Back in the day, pens were used to for writing letters and school work. So the used to run out fairly quick. My favorite disposable pen of all time might have been the papermate flexgrip. I had one that lasted over a decade and a half that I used for just writing checks. Well, that one finally ran out and I have been using odd pens here an there.
I don't write than much nowadays and was not familiar with a gel pen. I had a chance to test these out to see what that was about.
These come with a little protective nib tip that has to be removed before using. I guess this is somewhere between a wet rollerball and a thick ink pen. The ink flows smoothly and does not skip. If you use these for sketching, the line weight is actually pretty easy to control. The blue is a much darker blue and somewhere between blue and black.
Overall, the size and weight feel pretty good. These are comfortable pens. And they write smoothly and consistently.

reviews.title: They’re pens. They write well. Or is it good.
reviews.text:
These are “gel” pens. Pen is a pen is a pen….
But it’s by Sharpie. I’ve been a pen freak since childhood. Their looks, the way they write, the feel in my hand. I like this pen. I bought a 4 pack on a whim. It’s comfortable, has a nice even flow of ink and is retractable. I can’t say anything on ink drying time. It’s not a factor that makes or breaks a pen for me. I would purchase these pens again. Sharpie you have certainly come a long way from being a smelly black marker.

reviews.title: Smooth writing, but major quality issues.
reviews.text: I bought a bunch of these In all the different colors last year and the writing action really is the smoothest ever. However, the body of the pen is unmitigated crap. I have been using these for about a year now and the thread is not holding up against the pressure of the pen when it is in writing mode. The bodies are now falling apart on me, one after another. When I try to screw them back together, they just fall apart again. I find myself saving the insert to use later in whichever of the casings happens to withstand the test of time. However, this means I will have to also be sure to somehow let myself know what color the ink is in the altered pens. I can slide the color-coded silicone sleeve off of the faulty pen and install it on the other, but that will not look so cool, since the top of the pens are also color-coded and so won't end up matching unless I am "lucky" enough to get a working casing that matches the color of the insert. Huge disappointment that this product's body is of such inferior workmanship. I do so love how the pen writes...

reviews.title: Love These Pen(s)tels
reviews.text:
I absolutely adore these pens. They make writing fun! I decided I needed something different at work. Something that would make all the writing I do stand out in a very helpful way.

I go from one project to another regularly, not always able to finish one before something with a higher priority comes up. Using a different color each time I mark down what I'd just been working on. I can easily distinguish between all that I've written down at a glance with the rainbow of colors in my notebook.

The ink doesn't bleed through to the back of the page so I get to use both sides of the page and easily read it. I have a lot going on at work and making things more interesting with my different colored Pentels - actually helps me reduce stress by making stop for a few seconds each time I pick a new gel pen color. Those seconds add up over the course of each half hour. Since I have a habit of not taking breaks - these seemingly inconsequential moments of selecting a new color is soothing.

Being able to look over a page and see the fluidity of my writing broken only by the ink color shows me at a glance how much I have actually accomplished. I might have to Subscribe to these pens because I use them all the time now instead of just occasionally. I'll reevaluate things in a couple months. Stay tuned 🙂.

reviews.title: Labels don't stick and printing ability doesn't last.
reviews.text:
We've had this label maker for a few years, but only use it maybe once a or twice a year. I tried using it a bunch when we moved to label our plastic tubs, but as others have noted, the labels don't stick to anything but paper. Which is kind of useless. So I ended up printing labels and using packaging tape to tape over it.

What irritates me more is the whole "doesn't need ink!" claim, like it'll last forever as long as you get more tape. After my printer started doing a shoddy print job, I looked up on how to get more ink and found out there's no ink or ribbon to be had. So I looked up instructions on how to fix this--and Dymo said I simply had to "clean" the printer parts with this little wand thing inside the cap. Ok. So I cleaned it as per instructions, and when that didn't work I tried every other way under the sun and it did absolutely nothing to help the problem. I have a whole reel of tape going to go to waste because of this.

Needless to say, I won't be buying another Dymo to replace this one.

reviews.title: Smooth writing; nice grip
reviews.text: I have used gel pens for years as they are so much nicer than other types of pens.  I never tried Sharpie Brand, but like all the other Sharpie products, these are good quality.  They write smoothly and have a comfortable grip.  The only thing that might keep you from buying these is the price.  All gel pens tend to be a little pricey.  You can probably find cheaper gel pens, but you get what you pay for and may be disappointed with cheaper versions.  If you have never used a gel pen, give yourself a treat and get some.  Just realize that gel pens are not ideal for filling out multi-part forms.  You need to press hard to do that type of forms and gel pens don't require much pressure to use them.  In fact they are probably ideal for people who have limited hand strength and can't easily use another type of pen.

reviews.title: My preferred ink
reviews.text:
I've tried a large number of inks and have settled on this brand. I also use the 4001 black ink with the exact results, so it's the formula and not the pigment that make this so versatile. I've used this ink in a wide range of pens from modern Pelikans, Montblancs and others to vintage pens that are turning 100 like my Parkers and Watermans. It works beautifully with each of them.

This ink immediately starts on the first stroke and has never clogged nor prematurely dried out on a nib, even after extensive writing and letting the pen sit uncapped for prolonged periods. This is a somewhat dry ink that works best on slicker paper, although it writes beautifully on just about any surface from high rag papers to card stock. In fact, I just tested it on the highly absorbent paper in this journal and it worked perfectly: [[ASIN:B00Q1QACJG VALERY Rustic Genuine Leather Handcrafted Writing Blank Journal Diary With Handmade Paper]].

Because of the dry qualities it has minimum bleed on pulp type papers and even on thin sheets does not show through if you are using light strokes. It's not so dry that it's scratchy. On the contrary, this ink allows your nib to practically glide over most paper types.

reviews.title: My 'go to' ink
reviews.text:
I purchase mine locally, so am going to sidestep pricing factors and focus on the ink itself. I've used this ink in a wide range of pens from modern Pelikans, Montblancs and others to vintage pens that are turning 100 like my Parkers and Watermans. It works beautifully with each of them. I'll admit that my daily writer is a Pelikan M400 with a medium tip so my comments and observations will be based on that combination.

This ink immediately starts on the first stroke and has never clogged nor prematurely dried out on a nib, even after extensive writing and letting the pen sit uncapped for prolonged periods. This is a somewhat dry ink that works best on slicker paper, although it writes beautifully on just about any surface from high rag papers to card stock.

Because of the dry qualities it has minimum bleed on pulp type papers and even on thin sheets does not show through if you are using light strokes. It's not so dry that it's scratchy. On the contrary, this ink allows your nib to practically glide over most paper types.

I've tried a large number of inks and have settled on this brand. I also use the 4001 black ink with the exact results, so it's the formula and not the pigment that make this so versatile. One final note: this ink will keep for over a decade. I recently found a bottle I had forgotten about since opening it in 2004 and it works like I just opened it. In fact that is what inspired me to write this review.

reviews.title: My favorite for a long time.
reviews.text:
This has been my favorite ink since college. Lately every piece of paper I write on seems to get wet one way or another, so this isn't working as well for me. Next I'm going to try Noodler's American Eel, which I hope will be at least utile, if not as beautiful.

Pelikan 4001 does look wonderful and writes great. It is lubricated and its shading is nice. I love it in vintage Waterman flex pens. I'm finding it hard to hold onto the idea that I need to try something else, but the water solubility is getting to be a deal-breaker for me.

reviews.title: None of them came broken
reviews.text:
None of these came broken, which was unexpected and delightful and uncommon. I've gotten other brands and usually at least one is snapped in half if not more.

I use these for loose, impressionistic drawing or shading large areas, and they are great for that. They lay a nice, thick line, very smooth, easy to blend. I'm able to get a nice fuzzy line extending the point pretty far past the end of the holder and then turning the whole on it's side and going softly. Good for random shading in the grass, or bits of clouds. Or secure it properly and use the point for a somewhat crisper line. I can sand the tip with no problem, both to get dust to rub into the page as a foundation for a reductive drawing and to sharpen back the point to its original state.

I may try their 4B next. I went with the 2B because, as I said, I'm used to them coming broken and 2B is a good compromise between a dark line and a sturdy lead, but given how well these showed up, I might risk going for something softer.

These exceeded my expectations. Five stars.

Update: While I still love the 2B, the 4B came mostly broken. About half were in one piece. I would advise sticking with the 2B.

reviews.title: Nice looking, but not super functional
reviews.text:
CONTEXT
I bought these to replace the common (and unappealing) black mesh/wire ones that are commonly found in offices.

PROS
- These are very affordable! It was only $6/tray versus $10 or more!
- They are beautiful and modern. I love that they are white and very modern looking.
- Great quality! They're a very thick plastic (or whatever material it is) and are very sturdy and strong. Love that.

CONS
- Aside from looking good, they're really not that functional, at least for me. I use these trays for my invoices and when the bottom tray fills up, it's a hassle to pull out all of my invoices. What typically happens is, I'll end up pushing all the invoices to the back. The plastic is slippery, so I end up only grabbing a couple of invoices at a time. Trying to put my fingers under the paper is a bit difficult without hitting the top letter tray. So, I usually end up just pulling off the top tray and then grabbing my invoices. Some may see this as not that big of a deal, but I went from an easy to use letter tray to difficult to use, so there's that.
- The only other con is that the tray does not have rubber feet, so they slide around a lot on my very smooth desk.

OVERALL
The value for the product is definitely there. You can always add your own rubber feet to stop the slippage on your desk, but functionality-wise, I am not sure I would order these again. Even so, they do look good, so I guess there's that?

OVERALL

reviews.text: I wanted the smaller size, so that it would be useful as a daily planner and goals tracker, but I was worried that it might be tougher to write in due to how much smaller it is. Thank goodness that's not the case! I am pleased to report that this is a planner that really does make good on it's promise. The concept behind *this* planner is that large long-term goals can be achieved by breaking them down incrementally. The Penguin planner is an excellent option for short-term goals, but the Clever Fox is definitely designed for clarifying and achieving long-term plans in a fluid, and dynamic way. As with any change in habit, keeping up with the things you need to do is key to getting the job done, this planner helps you lay it all out, and helps you learn - in real time - where the log jams show up.

reviews.title: Jam-Free, But Stapling Takes More Effort
reviews.text:
When I saw these at a local Staples store, the promise of "jam free" stapling was irresistible. I purchased a box, which cost about a dollar more than a box of my usual Swingline S.F.4 chisel point staples. Both boxes appeared to be about the same size, although the Optima box has 3750 staples, and the S.F.4 box has 5000 staples.

The Optima staples do seem to be jam free, but stapling with them requires a lot more effort than stapling with S.F.4 staples. Probably the Optima staples are stuck together with special glue that prevents jamming, but doesn't want to let go. I used the Optima staples in a [[ASIN:B00006XY3P Swingline Compact Commercial Stapler (S7071101R)]], which is a terrific little desktop stapler. Maybe the Optima staples take less stapling effort when they're used in a larger stapler specifically designed for Optima staples.

For the future, I'm definitely sticking with the [[ASIN:B003RL7RRS Swingline S.F. 4 Premium Chisel Point Staples]]. The S.F.4 staples rarely jam, the sharp tips easily penetrate multiple sheets of paper, and they cost quite a bit less per box and per staple.

It's just that it's not leather, and the fourth word in is 'leather'. In fact, I wouldn't want a leather desk pad. The only issue so far is that it takes a while for it to lie flat, depending on which side you prefer, but not too long. That was also expected, but it does eventually lie flat. It doesn't slip, but it doesn't stick. My mouse glides over it easily, and recognizes movement precisely, but my keyboard stays put. It'll keep the cheap surface of my cheap "desk" from wearing. And it's a nice, dark, red on the red side. I've so often bought dark red plastic items that turned out to be anywhere from bright red to pink (I have a pink phone case right now, because a real man can wear pink), but this is actually a nice, dark red on the red side. Not much else I can say, other than the price is right. I wish my phone case was this color, but it matches fairly well. It depends on the lighting.

reviews.title: Such a pleasure to write with --
reviews.text:
I'm a total pen nut, and if you opened every drawer and purse and looked all around the nooks and crannies of my home, you'd probably find 200. Probably more. (Along with at least a hundred Sharpies, but that's another story.) And I love vibrant color. These Energel pens are my favorite. They are a total joy to write with. The ink flows easily and the colors are gorgeous. With the rubber grip, they feel good in your hand. These are head and shoulders above products made by other manufacturers that look very similar.

There is a different Energel pen on the market that comes in a different tube style. It looks like it is silver-tone metal (thought I don't know that it is) rather than plastic. It looks and feels better to hold than this model, and it seems like it would withstand wear and tear a little better. [[ASIN:B0012G9VNS Pentel EnerGel Deluxe RTX Gel Ink Pens, 0.7 Millimeter Metal Tip, Assorted Ink Colors, 6-Pack (BL77BP6M)]]. But they write exactly the same. The ink colors are beautiful and flow effortlessly onto paper. I like the metal tip -- although if you prefer a finer line, you might want to look for EnerGel pens with a needle tip.

I am always happy when I am writing with an EnerGel pen -- highly recommended.

reviews.title: Smeared even for a right-hander
reviews.text:
I'm a lefty, so I thought I'd give this a try as I needed a new pen for doing the puzzles in the newspaper. I'm picky about pens because I like how it feels when I'm working through the puzzle or writing notes. I also tend to write neater when a pen writes a certain way.

My husband is a righty and he used this pen to make his grocery list. Yet his writing was smudged -- something that doesn't happen with ballpoint pens.

I now know I prefer ballpoint pens to gel pens. Gel pens write softer with ink gently spilling out as you write. Ballpoint pens roll and make a dent in the paper. This is a matter of preference and I didn't reflect it in the rating. Rather, the rating is based on the pen's performance and smearing is a big problem. The company says this pen has "fast-drying ink" and that's not the case here. It works well and the ink shows clearly from the first contact with the paper. (You know how some pens need a little nudge before they wake up. This one doesn't.)

I hope you found this review helpful and will show it with a quick click of yes. If you have unanswered questions, please comment so I can update the review with answers to your questions.

The colors aren't exactly *pastel*. They're not desaturated and 'light' in color. The colors are in fact pretty bright and pretty unique. They show up very well on white/off-white paper. The colors aren't "neon" either. If you're a pen collector person who loves different pen colors for drawing or notetaking, I think you'd be *very* happy with these.

A bonus is the ink seems to resist smearing with water, even though it's not stated on the package. Uniball is a favorite brand of mine, but these are becoming favorites, as well.

reviews.title: Beautiful but not comfy
reviews.text: I love the look and feel of this pen. It’s beautiful and weighty. But I find it slippery to hold and the cap doesn’t stay on when you post it. Also, if you post the cap it places too much weight on the back end.  It was my error to purchase a medium point, I find it way too thick and messy for writing. It tends to bleed and spread.  I’d like to try a fine point but Ive gotten the Pilot retro pen and like the way it writes much better.

reviews.title: attractive and very well made
reviews.text: a nice bold nib and a overall an attractive writing instrument, however, I would suggest that you get a bottle of good ink and use the bladder instead of those plastic tubes.

reviews.title: A beautiful pen that feels good and writes really nice!
reviews.text:
This is a beautiful pen. I've been writing with Pilot Metropolitan, and this is far superior and roughly at the same price point.

The pen is elegant and has a nice weight to it. The whole thing is constructed well and comes in a beautiful leather box with the pen, blue and black ink. Included with the pen, already installed is the converter to use with a bottle of ink of your choice. This is what I did because I prefer to use this over refills, but you do have refills on hand to keep in case you run out of bottled link.

The cap comes off and is replaced with a very sturdy feel. The pen is nicely weighted in your hand. It writes beautifully with wonderful ink flow and the nib just rolls like melted butter over the paper. Very elegant and a wonderful pen for newbies to fountain pens, or those more experienced. I got the green one, and the color is a wonderful glossy hunter green.

If you are thinking of a pen for yourself, or a pen for a gift, this is an impressive pen at this price point.

reviews.title: Good But Not Great
reviews.text: I am a big fan of fountain pens and use them on a regular basis.  This pen is okay, but has a drag that can become annoying if writing a long letter.  The color is a major turn off, in the picture online it looked like "velvet" and that was what caught my eye. The pen is purple, but not a good shade of purple.  Very easy to change ink cartridges and having the converter is nice when I want to use bottled ink.  All in all it is a good pen for the money, but it isn't my go to pen.
